title: The Job Interview

jokes.text: we discovered a new breed of fly that mates with itself. Yummm! Mm.
jokes.setup: Ted shares his exciting scientific discovery about flies
jokes.punchline: Alexis responds with sexual enthusiasm to something inherently unsexy
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Ted's nerdy enthusiasm meets Alexis's misplaced sexuality. The specificity of 'mates with itself' makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I've discovered that flies can taste with their feet.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, that's so hot.

jokes.text: I've actually been bench pressing 100-pound tortoises because there's no gym here
jokes.setup: Alexis needs to explain how she's staying fit
jokes.punchline: She's using endangered animals as gym equipment
jokes.explanation: Perfectly absurd and character-specific. Only Alexis would casually mention using tortoises as weights with such specificity.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm using endangered animals as gym equipment. I do squats with baby pandas, and I use a slow loris for my bicep curls.

jokes.text: Ted! Stop making me jealous of turtles. Tortoises, but I do have tur-tell you
jokes.setup: Ted corrects turtle vs tortoise
jokes.punchline: Immediately makes a terrible 'turtle/tell' pun
jokes.explanation: Standard dad joke quality pun, but fits Ted's earnest character. The correction-to-pun flow works structurally.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Actually, it's a tortoise, not a turtle.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: You could say I'm really coming out of my shell with these facts!

jokes.text: Oh my God, David! Knock much?! / Hi, David! / Hi. Is he dressed?
jokes.setup: David walks in on Alexis and Ted's video call
jokes.punchline: David's only concern is whether Ted is clothed, while Ted cheerfully greets him
jokes.explanation: Great character dynamics - David's practical awkwardness vs Ted's oblivious friendliness. Economy in the exchange.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Please tell me you're wearing pants.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Hi, David!

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: He is.

jokes.text: You and Alexis were about to engage in cyber-sex. / Hi, Ted. / Hi, Mr. Rose.
jokes.setup: David matter-of-factly announces what Alexis and Ted were doing
jokes.punchline: Ted politely greets Johnny as if nothing awkward was just said
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe comedy - the contrast between David's bluntness and Ted's oblivious politeness. Well-structured escalation.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Ted and Alexis were just making out.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Hello, Mr. Rose.

jokes.text: Alexis, what have I told you about putting your body on the internet? Never! Never without proper lighting.
jokes.setup: Johnny seems to be giving moral guidance about internet safety
jokes.punchline: Moira reveals their only concern is aesthetic/professional
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection and perfect character writing. Classic Rose family priorities - vanity over morality. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You know, the internet is a dangerous place. You have to be very careful about what you post online. People can see it, they can screenshot it, they can use it against you.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, well, my only concern is that my lighting is absolutely dreadful in that photo.

jokes.text: Everybody out so I can finish off with Ted! Sick! Ugh! Goodbye, please!
jokes.setup: Alexis wants privacy for her call
jokes.punchline: Her phrasing is unfortunately sexual, David reacts with disgust
jokes.explanation: Standard sexual double entendre, but fits the scene's escalating awkwardness. David's disgusted reaction sells it.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Can everyone please leave? I need some privacy for this call.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I need you all to get out so I can take this long and hard.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God.

jokes.text: Okay, 'Larrierre' sounds like a dollar-store perfume. It's an airline. Larry is the name of the CEO.
jokes.setup: David mispronounces the airline name as something fancy
jokes.punchline: Reality is much more mundane - it's just named after a guy named Larry
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor about low-budget airlines. David's pretentious assumption vs mundane reality works well.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I was flying Air Français the other day, and I thought, 'How wonderfully sophisticated.'

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It's Air Canada.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, well, I was being generous with the pronunciation.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Also, it's named after a guy named Larry.

jokes.text: He used to own a chain of delis, but then he sold them to buy a bunch of planes.
jokes.setup: Learning about Larry's background
jokes.punchline: The casual transition from deli owner to airline owner
jokes.explanation: Absurd but believable backstory that explains why this airline would be terrible. Good worldbuilding humor.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: So your dad went from owning a deli to owning an airline?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Just like that?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, not just like that. There were several years in between.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: What happened in those years?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: He owned other things.

jokes.text: You know that I was once told that I would make an excellent flight attendant. By who? Sandy, the hostess on our private plane.
jokes.setup: David claims he'd make an excellent flight attendant
jokes.punchline: His only reference is from when he was a rich passenger
jokes.explanation: Perfect David moment - completely out of touch with reality, citing his privileged experience as qualification for service work.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I would make an excellent flight attendant. I have impeccable timing, grace under pressure, and I look phenomenal in a uniform.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: What's your experience?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I've flown first class extensively.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: That's not experience being a flight attendant, that's experience being a passenger.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, but I was a very demanding passenger, so I observed their technique closely.

jokes.text: She once let me debone a branzino during turbulence.
jokes.setup: David explaining his flight attendant qualifications
jokes.punchline: His 'experience' is absurdly specific rich person activity
jokes.explanation: Hilariously specific and absurd. Only David would think deboning fancy fish during turbulence qualifies him for airline work. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I have extensive experience in hospitality. I once arranged for a private jet to be stocked with only white truffles and Evian water that had been blessed by a shaman in Iceland.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I also coordinated a dinner service at 35,000 feet where every course was a different shade of beige, which, as you know, is the only acceptable color palette for in-flight dining.

jokes.text: if it's between the two of us, we all know who's getting the job. / if this is what we're working with, I think we can hand me the contract today.
jokes.setup: David's arrogant assumption about getting the job
jokes.punchline: Visual reveal of Stevie looking more professional while David looks ridiculous
jokes.explanation: Good setup for David's overconfidence vs reality. The visual component (his poor styling) adds to the joke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm obviously going to get this job. I mean, look at me.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm going to get this job.

jokes.text: So, you're Stevie Budd? No. Believe it or not, that's actually a woman's name.
jokes.setup: Confusion over the motel ownership
jokes.punchline: Johnny's matter-of-fact explanation about the gendered name
jokes.explanation: Mild observational humor about gender assumptions. Johnny's deadpan delivery helps but it's not particularly original.

dialogue.speaker: Bank Manager
dialogue.line: So you're telling me the motel is called the Rosebud?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Bank Manager
dialogue.line: And you own it?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I do.

dialogue.speaker: Bank Manager
dialogue.line: A man owning a motel called the Rosebud?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, it came with the name. I'm not going to change it just because it has a feminine quality to it. That would be small-minded.

jokes.text: Yeah, and look at the way this guy bounced back from total financial ruin.
jokes.setup: Roland trying to help Johnny's case with the bank
jokes.punchline: Accidentally highlights Johnny's biggest failure instead
jokes.explanation: Perfect Roland moment - trying to help but spectacularly missing the mark. Good ironic structure.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know what, Johnny? I'm gonna help you with the bank. I'll tell 'em about all your successful business ventures.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Like that time you invested in that... what was it... that thing that went belly up in three weeks?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, that's not helping.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: No, no, I got this. Or how about that other business idea that lost you half a million dollars?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Okay, stop.

jokes.text: We don't offer faith-based loans. / I am the mayor. You already mentioned that. / did I mention I went steady with your mom? Unfortunately, you did.
jokes.setup: Roland desperately trying different angles for influence
jokes.punchline: Each attempt is more inappropriate and already failed
jokes.explanation: Great escalating desperation from Roland, with the banker's increasingly weary responses. Good cringe comedy structure.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Look, I'm a very important man in this town. I sit on the town council, I'm a member of the Rotary Club, and I know the mayor personally.

dialogue.speaker: Bank Manager
dialogue.line: That's nice, but it doesn't change your credit score.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, what if I told you my son is dating your daughter?

dialogue.speaker: Bank Manager
dialogue.line: I don't have a daughter.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: What if you did? Think about it.

dialogue.speaker: Bank Manager
dialogue.line: I'm going to have to ask you to leave.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Okay, okay. One more thing. How much would it take for this to go away? Hypothetically.

dialogue.speaker: Bank Manager
dialogue.line: That's called bribery.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, I didn't say it had to be money.

jokes.text: Okay, then I'm out. I got nothing.
jokes.setup: Roland's failed attempts at influence
jokes.punchline: His immediate, complete surrender
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflated ending to Roland's ridiculous attempts. The sudden honesty is funny.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, I have influence in this town. People respect me.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, last week you tried to get a discount at the gas station and they laughed you out.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: That was different. I wasn't trying hard enough. This time I'm going to show them what real power looks like.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Okay, fine. You're right. I have no influence. Can we just go home?

jokes.text: Please tell me it's not a testudine. No, it's a turtle.
jokes.setup: Moira uses the scientific term for turtle
jokes.punchline: Alexis completely misses it and gives the simple answer
jokes.explanation: Good character contrast - Moira's pretentious vocabulary vs Alexis's simplicity. Shows their communication gap.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Did you know that a testudine is a fascinating creature?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, a what? Is that like a disease?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No, darling. It's a turtle.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: A turtle? Why would you say testudine? Just say turtle!

jokes.text: Turtles do not pets make. The poor things are riddled with a myriad of diseases. You may as well tie a leash around a raw chicken cutlet.
jokes.setup: Moira explaining why turtles are bad pets
jokes.punchline: The bizarrely specific 'raw chicken cutlet' comparison
jokes.explanation: Classic Moira - dramatic, unnecessarily specific, and vaguely disturbing. The chicken cutlet image is memorably gross and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Turtles are terrible pets. They live for decades, they carry salmonella, and quite frankly, holding one feels like you're cradling a raw chicken cutlet.

jokes.text: Relax, they said he's already been washed.
jokes.setup: Moira's health concerns about the turtle
jokes.punchline: Alexis thinks washing solves all disease concerns
jokes.explanation: Perfect Alexis logic - completely missing the point while being confidently wrong. The innocence makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm concerned about the turtle's health. These creatures can carry all sorts of diseases.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Just wash your hands. I wash my hands like, all the time, and I'm fine.

jokes.text: I've named the turtle Ted. All the blogs about successful long-term relationships say that the key is creating shared experiences.
jokes.setup: Alexis explaining the turtle's name
jokes.punchline: She thinks naming a pet after her boyfriend counts as a 'shared experience'
jokes.explanation: Hilariously misguided relationship advice application. Classic Alexis taking something literally in the wrong way.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: She thinks naming a pet after her boyfriend counts as a 'shared experience'

jokes.text: if you desire a love with longevity, the key is to have as little in common as possible. After an award-worthy trilogy of decades together, your father and I still astonish each other.
jokes.setup: Moira giving relationship advice
jokes.punchline: Her advice is backwards, and she describes marriage like a film trilogy
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moira vocabulary and perspective. 'Award-worthy trilogy of decades' is such a Moira way to describe a marriage.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Marriage is like a film trilogy. The first one is passionate and unexpected, the second one is slightly disappointing, and by the third one, you're just hoping it ends.

jokes.text: So you wanna be a youth pastor? / While that joke was surprisingly sharp, these are Patrick's clothes.
jokes.setup: David's conservative outfit choice
jokes.punchline: David's defensive response that credits the joke while defending his fashion choice
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - David simultaneously acknowledging the burn while deflecting responsibility. Shows his vanity.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: That's a very... conservative outfit choice.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I think it's funny that you would say that, because this is actually a very well-tailored, expensive piece of clothing.

jokes.text: While, that joke was surprisingly sharp, these are Patrick's clothes.
jokes.setup: Stevie's youth pastor comment
jokes.punchline: David compliments the joke while defending his outfit choice
jokes.explanation: Nice meta moment where David acknowledges the quality of the burn against him

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's a good joke. But I want to be clear, I wasn't dressing like a youth pastor. I was dressing like someone who has a very nice life and a great sense of style.

jokes.text: Larry Air, recent improvements: all chairs will have tray tables, not just first class. I feel like I'm flying on the Concorde.
jokes.setup: Reading about Larry Air's 'improvements'
jokes.punchline: The bare minimum amenity is treated as luxury, David's sarcastic comparison
jokes.explanation: Good satire of budget airlines treating basic amenities as upgrades. David's sarcasm fits well.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, they've added a phone in the room. How luxurious. I suppose next they'll be installing indoor plumbing and electricity as standard features.

jokes.text: if anyone here is involved in the class-action lawsuit against Larry Air, you are disqualified from applying.
jokes.setup: Standard job interview opening
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of legal troubles at a job interview
jokes.explanation: Good worldbuilding joke about how terrible this airline is. The casualness of mentioning lawsuits is funny.

dialogue.speaker: Carol
dialogue.line: So, I see here you have some legal troubles on your record...

dialogue.speaker: Applicant
dialogue.line: Yes, well, it was all a misunderstanding.

dialogue.speaker: Carol
dialogue.line: It says here you were arrested for fraud, embezzlement, and tax evasion.

dialogue.speaker: Applicant
dialogue.line: Like I said, misunderstandings.

jokes.text: I'd say my biggest weakness is... that I'm disorganized. Ooh, that's true. And, um, I'm indecisive. Yes. Uh... I also... Okay. We just needed one.
jokes.setup: Standard interview question about weaknesses
jokes.punchline: Stevie demonstrates her weaknesses by listing too many and being indecisive, David confirms each one
jokes.explanation: Brilliant demonstration over description. Stevie literally shows her weaknesses while trying to describe them, and David making it worse is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm a perfectionist. I'm too detail-oriented. I'm not a great delegator. I can be impatient. I'm not always the best communicator. I second-guess myself sometimes. I can be a little stubborn. I'm not great with change. I tend to hold grudges. I'm not always—

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yeah, no, I think we've got it.

jokes.text: Would we consider loyalty a weakness? If not, I'm also really honest. I'm just gonna put down chatty.
jokes.setup: David trying to game the weakness question
jokes.punchline: His 'strengths as weaknesses' fail, interviewer sees right through him
jokes.explanation: Classic David overconfidence meets reality. Carol's blunt assessment is the perfect puncture of his smugness.

dialogue.speaker: Interviewer
dialogue.line: So, David, what would you say is your greatest weakness?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I'd have to say I'm a perfectionist. I care too much about my work, and I hold myself to impossibly high standards.

dialogue.speaker: Interviewer
dialogue.line: I see. And is that actually a weakness, or are you just describing a strength?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I... well, it can be both.

dialogue.speaker: Interviewer
dialogue.line: No, it really can't.

jokes.text: Roland, you're not adding anything to the sentence, you're just repeating what I said.
jokes.setup: Roland trying to be helpful in conversation
jokes.punchline: Johnny's frustrated observation about Roland's conversational style
jokes.explanation: Perfect meta-commentary on Roland's annoying habit. Johnny's exasperation is relatable and well-earned.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, every time you open your mouth, it's like you're doing us all a favor by sharing your thoughts. But the truth is, you're just making it worse.

jokes.text: the other day, I got a look at Bob's financial statement on his desk in the office, and that garage is doing great! Plus, he got plugs last year, but... you didn't hear that from me.
jokes.setup: Roland sharing inside information about Bob's finances
jokes.punchline: Immediately follows with gossip about hair plugs and pretends discretion
jokes.explanation: Classic Roland gossip behavior. The hair plugs detail and fake discretion are perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Now, I probably shouldn't be telling you this, but Bob's finances are in worse shape than his hairline.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: And between you and me, those hair plugs cost him forty grand. But obviously, I'm very discreet about these things.

jokes.text: Oh, look at you, Robert, with the hides of a herd of Holstein on your back.
jokes.setup: Moira greeting Bob
jokes.punchline: Her overly elaborate way of saying he's wearing leather
jokes.explanation: Peak Moira vocabulary - turning 'nice leather jacket' into something that sounds biblical. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Bob, darling, I see you've decided to drape yourself in the skin of a deceased bovine. How very... tactile of you.

jokes.text: I just had two cups of coffee, I don't wanna push my luck. I mean, these pants, they take like a year to unlace, so...
jokes.setup: Bob needing to use the bathroom
jokes.punchline: TMI about his leather pants and bathroom urgency
jokes.explanation: Standard TMI humor. The leather pants detail adds some specificity but it's not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: I need to use the bathroom. These leather pants are cutting off my circulation.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's... more information than I needed.

jokes.text: Rose's, I hope you like fish, 'cause I think we just reeled in a big one.
jokes.setup: Roland thinking they've found their investor
jokes.punchline: Terrible fishing pun while completely misreading the situation
jokes.explanation: Typical Roland bad pun plus his overconfidence. The joke is that Roland thinks he's being clever.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, well, well. Looks like we've got ourselves a biter on the line. This investor's gonna be easy to reel in.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I can already sea the dollar signs. We're gonna have a real catch of a deal here.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: This one's gonna be a keeper, mark my words.

jokes.text: it's been weeks. But between the patchy Wi-Fi and like the ten-day nature trips, and me being alone here, kind of feels like he left the day that he flew to the Galapagos.
jokes.setup: Alexis explaining when she last saw the turtle
jokes.punchline: The timeline reveals she's conflating the turtle's escape with her boyfriend leaving
jokes.explanation: Good emotional logic from Alexis - the turtle and boyfriend absence feel connected to her. Shows her emotional processing.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I think it was... it was right after David and I broke up. Or maybe right before? No, it was definitely around the same time as when the turtle got out.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Actually, now that I think about it, I'm not entirely sure which happened first — David leaving or the turtle escaping. They kind of blur together in my mind.

jokes.text: The turtle flew to the Galapagos? No, Ted the turtle is missing, I am now talking about my boyfriend Ted, Twy. It'd be great if you could keep up.
jokes.setup: Confusion about which Ted Alexis is discussing
jokes.punchline: Alexis's condescending response to perfectly reasonable confusion she created
jokes.explanation: Perfect Alexis moment - creating confusion then blaming others for not following her logic. Great callback structure.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Wait, which Ted are we talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Um, the Ted that I'm dating? There's only one Ted in my life, Twyla.

jokes.text: The slowest animal on earth escaped from me. That can't not be a bad sign.
jokes.setup: Alexis reflecting on losing the turtle
jokes.punchline: Self-aware observation about what this says about her
jokes.explanation: Brilliant self-deprecating insight from Alexis. The logic is sound and funny, with perfect economy. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I just realized I lost a turtle. Like, I had a living creature in my care and I completely forgot about it. What does that say about me?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, I can't even keep a pet alive. How am I supposed to keep myself together?

jokes.text: My mom had a turtle dove that was eaten by her ex-fiancé's snake. That was a bad sign. And it happened on Valentine's Day. And her fiancé was a Satanist.
jokes.setup: Twyla trying to be supportive with a similar story
jokes.punchline: Each detail makes the story more absurd and inappropriate
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalating absurdity with great structure. Each reveal makes it worse. Classic Twyla oblivious oversharing.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Oh, that happened to me once! Well, not exactly like that, but similar. I was at the food court, and I sat down at what I thought was my table, but it turned out to be someone else's table.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: And then security came, and they thought I was stealing french fries, so they tackled me.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Turns out it was the mayor's table, and I landed on his wife, and she went into early labor.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: The baby was fine, but the mayor banned me from the mall for life. And also from the hospital. And also I'm not allowed to be within 100 yards of his family.

jokes.text: Oh, and if you do see Ted like waddling around in the kitchen, just feed him a carrot and shoot me a text. I hope you're joking. Me too, girl. Love you!
jokes.setup: Alexis asking Twyla to watch for the turtle
jokes.punchline: Her casual attitude about a turtle in a restaurant kitchen
jokes.explanation: Good health code violation humor. Alexis's obliviousness to the restaurant implications is in character.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Twyla, can you just keep an eye out for the turtle?

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: A turtle? In the kitchen?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yes, it's fine. Just don't let it get near the walk-in or anything.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Okay... I'll just add that to my list of things to worry about.

jokes.text: This baby behind me has been crying for over an hour now and I just can't take it anymore. / I have shushed them thrice at this point.
jokes.setup: Flight attendant roleplay scenario
jokes.punchline: David's inappropriate response and formal vocabulary ('thrice')
jokes.explanation: Perfect David customer service - formal language but completely inappropriate. Using 'thrice' while complaining about babies is very him.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, but I've already been upgraded thrice this month. I don't think that's physically possible.

jokes.text: I could either move those two to the back of the plane, or I could offer you some complimentary alcohol to make your flight a little more bearable.
jokes.setup: David offering solutions to crying baby problem
jokes.punchline: Both solutions are completely against airline policy
jokes.explanation: Good setup for David's complete misunderstanding of the job. His solutions sound helpful but are totally wrong.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, have you tried giving the baby a sedative? Or perhaps just opening the emergency exit?

jokes.text: no seat changes, and 'complimentary' is not in our corporate vocabulary. But finger snaps for David! We don't... Sure.
jokes.setup: Carol correcting David's airline policy mistakes
jokes.punchline: David's confused response to the condescending 'finger snaps' praise
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment. David's confusion about the patronizing praise and weird finger snaps gesture is awkward and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Carol
dialogue.line: David, you don't understand airline policy at all.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I—

dialogue.speaker: Carol
dialogue.line: No, no, no. *snap snap* Good effort though.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, are you snapping at me like I'm a golden retriever?

jokes.text: Fuck yes! I regret to inform you, you will not be asked to continue the process. This is so embarrassing. I thought you said David Rose. I did.
jokes.setup: David celebrates hearing his name called
jokes.punchline: He's actually being eliminated, then compounds the embarrassment
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection and escalating embarrassment. David's celebration followed by humiliation is peak cringe comedy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God, they called my name!

dialogue.speaker: Host
dialogue.line: David Rose, you're safe.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I know, I heard—wait, what?

dialogue.speaker: Host
dialogue.line: You're eliminated.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: But you just said I was safe!

dialogue.speaker: Host
dialogue.line: No, I said you're safe to leave. You're out.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, this is humiliating.

jokes.text: Too bad they didn't have a branzino to de-bone because you would've got this.
jokes.setup: Stevie commenting on David's rejection
jokes.punchline: References his earlier absurd qualification claim
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback to David's earlier ridiculous claim. Stevie's deadpan delivery while he's down is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, he said I didn't meet his standards. Which is rich coming from a man who once told me his only qualification was that he could open a door.

jokes.text: some of us drove some of the other applicants here, so it might just be easier if we all just moved forward into the next round.
jokes.setup: David trying to negotiate staying in the interview
jokes.punchline: His completely illogical argument for group advancement
jokes.explanation: Classic David entitlement - trying to use carpooling as leverage for job consideration. The logic is hilariously flawed.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Look, I understand your concerns, but if you think about it logically, we're actually better off staying together as a group.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Because if one of us leaves, we're no longer a complete group, which means we've all failed individually.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So really, the only way for any of us to succeed is if we all stay exactly where we are. It's simple mathematics.

jokes.text: I'm taking the peanuts, Carol!
jokes.setup: Carol explaining nothing is complimentary
jokes.punchline: David's petty rebellion with aggressive peanut theft
jokes.explanation: Perfect David tantrum moment. The peanut theft as final act of defiance is petty and funny. Good escalation.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm taking the peanuts.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: All of them.

jokes.text: How often do good friends get a chance to sit around and, uh, and talk? Right! And, you know, I didn't get a chance to tell you before, but that is one snazzy outfit.
jokes.setup: Roland trying to butter up Bob for money
jokes.punchline: Awkward, transparent flattery about Bob's expensive clothes
jokes.explanation: Classic cringe dinner conversation. Roland's obvious money-motivated flattery is painfully transparent.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Bob, those are some really expensive-looking pants. Are those Italian?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: They're from a catalogue.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: A very fancy catalogue.

jokes.text: This is so exciting! I feel like I'm a Wolf of Wall Street. Good... then let's go get our lamb.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn's excitement about potential investment
jokes.punchline: Moira's predatory metaphor about targeting Bob
jokes.explanation: Good contrast between Jocelyn's innocent enthusiasm and Moira's calculating predator metaphor. Shows their different approaches.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Oh my God, this is so exciting! A real business investment opportunity!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, we need to think like predators, Jocelyn. We circle. We isolate our prey. We move in for the kill.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: You mean Bob?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Exactly. Bob is a wounded gazelle, and we are very hungry lions.

jokes.text: John, were you able to return Bill and Melinda's call today about the hmm hmm-hmm? / Well, you know, Moira, we're not supposed to be discussing investment opportunities at the table, no matter how lucrative they might be. But you know what the Gateses are like - Bill and Melinda.
jokes.setup: Moira and Johnny performing fake wealth for Bob
jokes.punchline: Their over-the-top pretense about knowing Bill and Melinda Gates
jokes.explanation: Great con artist routine from the Roses. The fake casual name-dropping of the Gateses is perfectly executed desperation.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We're very close with Bill and Melinda Gates. We summer together.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Every summer.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We call them Billy and Mel.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Very casual relationship.

jokes.text: Gwen's not coming. Let's eat. / she left me. / I'm sorry, it's just all this, all this money talk, and all I can think about is how much I spent on this suit.
jokes.setup: The elaborate dinner scheme
jokes.punchline: Bob's personal crisis reveals the expensive suit was for the wrong reasons
jokes.explanation: Good ironic reversal - the expensive clothes they noticed were signs of desperation, not wealth. Subverts their assumptions.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: I bought this suit for the dinner because I thought it would impress the clients.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, you spent three thousand dollars on a suit?

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Well, yes, but—

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The dinner was cancelled an hour ago.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: What? No, I... I needed to look successful. I'm a failure, David. The business is failing, my marriage is—

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm standing right here, Robert.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: The point is, I thought if I looked the part, maybe I could finally feel like I wasn't letting everyone down.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, we don't care what suit you're wearing.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Well, I do.

jokes.text: she said, 'I like bad boys,' and now she's holed up with our minister. She was bragging about a religious experience.
jokes.setup: Bob explaining why Gwen left him
jokes.punchline: The minister is apparently the 'bad boy,' religious experience has double meaning
jokes.explanation: Good double entendre and irony about the minister being the 'bad boy.' The religious experience pun works well.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: She left me for the minister. Apparently he's the 'bad boy' type, and religious experience has a whole different meaning.

jokes.text: Perhaps there's an upside to all this, fiscally speaking. Hmm! Oh no, no.
jokes.setup: Bob's marriage ending
jokes.punchline: Moira immediately sees financial opportunity in his divorce
jokes.explanation: Perfectly ruthless Moira moment - seeing opportunity in Bob's pain. Johnny's horror is the perfect reaction beat.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Bob, darling, I heard about your marriage. I'm so sorry. But you know what this means? A fresh start! New beginnings! And more importantly, a chance to liquidate assets.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira...

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm thinking we could help him with the real estate side of things. The house sale, the settlement. We'd take a modest commission, of course.

jokes.text: You are such a sore loser. I am not! I'm not. It's just, do you really wanna work for a place that clearly doesn't know what they're doing?
jokes.setup: Stevie calling out David's behavior
jokes.punchline: David's immediate contradiction of his denial, then sour grapes rationalization
jokes.explanation: Classic David denial immediately followed by proof of what he's denying. The sour grapes rationalization is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You're being defensive.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not being defensive.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And I don't care what you think anyway.

jokes.text: Oh, well, they really want me to work for them, so I have to trust that they do know what they're doing. Admit it. I'm more qualified than you. Fine. Admit it. You're more qualified than me. Thank you!
jokes.setup: Stevie rubbing in her success
jokes.punchline: David's grudging admission extracted through repetition
jokes.explanation: Good sibling-like dynamic. Stevie forcing the admission and David's reluctant compliance feels very real.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm doing really well, David. Like, really well.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, I can see that.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Better than you expected?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I wouldn't say that.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Better than you're doing?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Stevie...

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Just say it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Fine. Yes. You're doing better than I am. Happy?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Thrilled.

jokes.text: let's see what exciting new job opportunities await you in your new career as an airline hostess. / I am so embarrassed for you! Now, I would love to use one of Larry Air's 'completely private' new paid toilets.
jokes.setup: David's petty revenge reading
jokes.punchline: More evidence of how terrible Larry Air is, while David claims to be embarrassed for Stevie
jokes.explanation: Good continued worldbuilding about Larry Air's awfulness. David's attempt to embarrass Stevie backfires as usual.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm reading a scathing review of Larry Air, and it's absolutely devastating. The reviewer calls their customer service 'aggressively unhelpful' and their planes 'flying coffins.'

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: David, why are you reading this to me?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Because you work there, and I'm genuinely embarrassed for you.

jokes.text: How do I know this isn't like an abduction or something? It's not an abduction. You didn't blink. I believe you.
jokes.setup: Alexis suspicious of the limo pickup
jokes.punchline: Her bizarre test for trustworthiness based on blinking
jokes.explanation: Perfect Alexis logic - a completely made-up lie detector test that she fully believes in. Original and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I need you to prove you're not a serial killer. Blink twice if you're a serial killer.

dialogue.speaker: Limo Driver
dialogue.line: I'm not blinking.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Exactly. Serial killers always blink. It's like their tell.

jokes.text: And you're wearing the little tie with your short-sleeved shirt. My little Galapo-guy. / Yeah, it's actually really hot here, so the bottom half is just my bathing suit and flip-flops.
jokes.setup: Alexis admiring Ted's formal appearance
jokes.punchline: Ted reveals he's only dressed up from the waist up for the video call
jokes.explanation: Classic video call humor about dressing up only what's visible. Ted's practicality vs romantic appearance is funny.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Ted, you look so handsome and put-together.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Thank you. Well, I should tell you, I'm only dressed from the waist up. I'm wearing sweatpants.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What? Why?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: It's a video call. No one can see below the desk.

jokes.text: Ted! More about your bottom half, please! / Twyla's not standing right behind you, is she? / Hi, Ted.
jokes.setup: Alexis making suggestive comment
jokes.punchline: Ted's awareness of being overheard, confirmed by Twyla's greeting
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy timing. Ted's awareness and Twyla's innocent greeting creates perfect awkwardness.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, he does have very nice hands.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Oh! Hi.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Hi Ted!

jokes.text: In fact, they actually named the new fly 'Alexis.' / The fly that mates with itself?
jokes.setup: Ted thinking he's being romantic by naming the fly after Alexis
jokes.punchline: Alexis remembers the unflattering detail about the fly's behavior
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback that gets funnier with context. Ted's romantic gesture becomes accidentally insulting.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I named the fly after you because it's beautiful and graceful.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, Ted, that fly eats garbage and throws up on its food before it digests it.

jokes.text: We... are buying into the business. Say hello to your new business partners. / So, we have the motel? We sure do. Now, first order of business, we need name tags.
jokes.setup: The big business announcement
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's immediate focus on the most trivial detail
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jocelyn moment - missing the big picture for silly details. Shows what working with them will be like.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We're opening a new location for the business. It's going to be a major expansion.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: That's wonderful news, Johnny! Congratulations!

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Oh my goodness, that's so exciting! But tell me—what color are the napkins going to be?

jokes.text: if it's good enough for Bill Gates to invest in, it's, uh, good enough for us to invest in.
jokes.setup: Roland explaining their investment decision
jokes.punchline: Taking Johnny's obvious lies as fact
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback showing how Johnny's lies backfired into unwanted partnership

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: So Johnny comes to me with this 'business opportunity,' right? Says he's got a sure thing. Guaranteed returns.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, I'm telling you, this is legitimate.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: And I believe him! Why wouldn't I? The man's never lied to me before.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Roland, Johnny lies constantly.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, I didn't know that at the time!

jokes.text: Why is there a turtle on the floor? Alexis!
jokes.setup: The celebration scene
jokes.punchline: The missing turtle has been found at the worst moment, Johnny's exasperated recognition
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback ending. The turtle's return during the celebration is great timing, and Johnny's immediate recognition is funny.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Of course the turtle shows up now.

tmdb_overview: Johnny and Moira endeavor to secure an investor for a new motel while Alexis and Ted struggle with long-distance.
insight: Character-driven cringe sustains a 77-point finale setup despite moderate joke density.
